_meta.description: Strategies 18–22 with long definitions added (v1.3). This is chunk 04 of the full set.

speaking: Students will orally {function} {content_focus} using {supports} (e.g., {frames}), producing {product} that meets {criteria}.
Students will discuss {content_focus} in pairs, using {frames} to {function} and incorporate {target_vocab}.
listening: Students will comprehend {input_type} on {content_focus} and demonstrate understanding by {response_mode} using {supports}.
Students will follow {procedural_input} and {function} (e.g., sequence, identify) the steps for {content_focus}.
reading: Students will read {text_type} about {content_focus} and {function} (e.g., identify, infer) key ideas using {strategy} and {target_vocab}.
Students will use {strategy} (e.g., cognates, text features) to make meaning from {text_type} about {content_focus}.
writing: Students will write a {genre} to {purpose} about {content_focus}, using {frames}/{target_vocab} and {language_features}.
Students will revise a short paragraph to {function} (e.g., explain, justify) understanding of {content_focus} using {criteria}.

speaking+listening: In structured talk, students will {function} about {content_focus}, using {frames} while peers listen and {response_mode}.
reading+writing: Students will read {text_type} on {content_focus} and write a {genre} that {purpose}, using {target_vocab} and {criteria}.
all: Through {framework}, students will listen to {input_type}, discuss {content_focus}, read {text_type}, and write a {genre} that {purpose}, with {supports}.

strategies.core_principle: Brief L1 preview and post-lesson review while main instruction occurs in English.
implementation_steps: Preview key ideas/vocabulary in L1; teach in English; review/clarify in L1.
Invite L1 explanations to check comprehension; recycle target English terms.
primary_benefits: Maintains lesson flow without constant translation
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Brief L1 preview of key ideas before an English lesson and short L1 review after, reinforcing comprehension.
definitions.long_en: Preview–Review is a bilingual scaffolding strategy where the teacher introduces two or three key concepts and essential vocabulary in students’ L1 before delivering the main lesson in English. The preview is short, focused, and supported by visuals or demonstrations. The main instruction proceeds in English, with scaffolds such as sentence frames or manipulatives. At the end, the teacher briefly reviews the concepts in L1, checks understanding, and immediately cycles back to the English terms to strengthen transfer. This approach lowers cognitive load, ensures access to critical ideas, and maintains English as the primary medium. It is especially useful when teaching abstract concepts, launching new units, or clarifying safety directions. The strategy should remain time-bounded and purposeful; if it expands into re-teaching the entire lesson in L1 or continuous translation, its effectiveness is lost.
look_fors: Concise L1 preview with visuals before lesson
English lesson delivered without ongoing translation
Brief L1 review and comprehension check after
English vocabulary recycled immediately after review
non_examples: Full re-teaching of lesson in L1
Extended L1 discussions unrelated to English output

strategies.core_principle: Framework of best practices to make grade-level content comprehensible while developing academic English.
implementation_steps: State content + language objectives; build background and vocabulary.
Use visuals/interactions; practice & application; review/assess for both domains.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: A comprehensive framework integrating content and language objectives with strategies for making input comprehensible.
definitions.long_en: The Sheltered Instruction Observation Protocol (SIOP) is a validated framework that guides teachers in planning and delivering lessons integrating content and language objectives. It includes eight components: lesson preparation, building background, comprehensible input, strategies, interaction, practice/application, lesson delivery, and review/assessment. Teachers explicitly state and review objectives, teach key vocabulary, and use techniques like visuals, modeling, and cooperative learning to make grade-level content accessible. Students engage in structured opportunities to practice new language orally and in writing. SIOP lessons balance content rigor with language scaffolds, ensuring equity for bilingual learners in mainstream classrooms. The model is widely researched and effective across grade levels. It should not be reduced to a checklist of strategies; fidelity requires coherent planning, reflection, and consistency. When fully implemented, SIOP helps close achievement gaps while accelerating academic English development.
look_fors: Content and language objectives posted and reviewed
Comprehensible input with visuals and modeling
Multiple opportunities for interaction and practice

strategies.core_principle: Provide temporary supports (models, visuals, frames) and fade as independence grows.
implementation_steps: Model (I do–We do–You do); use organizers and manipulatives.
Provide sentence frames/word banks; gradually remove supports.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Temporary supports (visuals, modeling, sentence frames) that enable learners to perform tasks just beyond independent ability.
definitions.long_en: Scaffolding refers to the instructional supports teachers provide to help students accomplish tasks they could not complete independently. Examples include modeling a process, using visuals, providing sentence starters, chunking texts, or guiding practice in small steps. Scaffolds are gradually withdrawn as students gain proficiency, fostering independence. For bilingual learners, scaffolds may include bilingual glossaries, L1 peer support, or translation of key terms, always with the goal of bridging to English academic language. Effective scaffolding requires diagnosing students’ current level, anticipating challenges, and matching supports to objectives. It is a dynamic process: supports must be adjusted based on observation. Scaffolding should not become permanent crutches; overuse can limit independence. Instead, supports fade strategically as students internalize skills and language.
look_fors: Use of visuals, sentence frames, or chunked texts
Supports matched to language and content goals
non_examples: Assigning complex tasks with no supports
Providing permanent sentence frames without fading
